text.heading: Immunity Clause: Remove it, pass FoI Bill
text.p: I would like to align myself very strongly with President Yar'Adua's position regarding removing the Immunity clause. I believe that the Immunity clause should be removed since the purpose it was designed to serve has not been served and it has instead become a barrier to vertical accountability, which actually is the very essence of the democratic process.

Indeed, this position that the President has expressed should traditionally go together with the institution of the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill because the removal of Executive Immunity and the implementation of the FOI law work together to facilitate accountability to the people, as this is what leads governments to performing.

Understandably, the original intent of the Immunity clause is to avoid frivolous civil suits. Frivolous in the sense that everybody can go to court claiming one thing or the other against somebody in public office, and that would get in the way of an officer concentrating.

It is supposed to check civil issues becoming a nuisance but we have interpreted it in such a way that it has become a problem now for democratic process, making way for deliberate violation of the laws of the land against the very essence of holding public office whereas such should be subject to instant prosecution.
I would therefore say President Yar'Adua is in the case setting the appropriate moral tone for the country.
We may find instruction in that President Clinton faced a barrage of different litigations as sitting-president yet none and all prevented him from doing his job as the President of America.
We can actually remove the Immunity clause completely and allow for some other ways whereby executive public officers settle their civil litigations."

Regarding institutions that the constitution actually adequately provides the relevant powers, it is saddening, the fact that most State House of Assemblies by remaining in the pockets of their Governors, for instance, have turned out as one of the tragedies of our democracy which is why there is such a high level of bad governance at the state level.

This situation is an opportunity to renew my call for a citizen-legislature because if we had citizen legislators where the state legislators and those at the National Assemblies is made up of sound professional people as successful lawyers in their chambers, artisans, or whatever they are, and then only take out time to make and review laws and they have oversight, they would probably be less amenable to purchase by state or federal executives and hence be less inclined to be willing tools of executive abuse.

I urge all to learn a huge lesson from the fact that ordinary average law enforcement agents can tap the phone of a Governor, as is the case of Illinois right now, overhear discussions of possibilities of dealings in a corrupt manner, and so begin attempts to prosecute almost immediately.
